title: UNIT २

text:
2.0 OBJECTIVES

In this unit we shall look at: the essential conflict between two egoistic leaders, the emergence of Achilles as the Homeric hero the comparisons and points of contrast between Achilles and Hector as the hero of the Iliad -OU 2.1 INTRODUCTION The Iliadic war started primilary because of the abduction of Queen Helen, the beautiful wife of the Greek King Menelaus of Sparta by the Trojan Prince, Paris . King Menelaus was the brother of King Agamemnon , King of Mycenae. OPLES The poem depicts only the last forty days of the war and bypasses the main cause of the conflict ( Helen $ abduction ) between the Trojans and the Greeks. It also leaves out the final outcome of the war- the Trojan rout by the Greeks RSITY with the help of the famous wooden horse. The Iliad in २४ books focuses on the Greek hero, Achilles who was responsible for the death of Hector, his Trojan counterpart and the rout of the Trojan army Achilles had initially withdrawn from the fight in anger against his chief Agamemnon s decision to claim Briseis , the slave woman, whom he ( Achilles ) had won as his war prize. While Agamemnon is the leader of the Greek forces, he depends on the heroic valour of Achilles to win battles for him. Hence, when Achilles is denied his prize slave, he is upset and refuses to fight on Agamemnon s side. Thus, the essential conflict at the beginning of the Iliad is the clash of two strong egos that of the leader, Agamemnon and Achilles. Agamemnon believes in his hierarchal position as leader and therefore feels justified in claiming the war booty from Achilles the greatest Greek warrior though he is a subordinate soldier Achilles feels insulted and humiliated, as he is far too conscious of his worth, his sense of personal glory and personal excellence- what the Greeks define a$ arete . Both believe in their own superiority and their own subjective notion of the pursuit of excellence and thus, ensues the conflict between them. The poem relates the heroic fight of Achilles that routes the Trojans and kills Hector, the Trojan prince. The next section will analyse Achilles as a warrior hero. Homer: The Iliad Check Your Progress _ Do you see any parallel between the Mahabharata and the Iliad in the context of the clash of two strong egos of Duryodhan and Dharma in the Mahabharata in the former and between Achilles and Agamemnon in the latter? U H0MCR TID0 6 2.2 ACHILLES, THE WARRIOR HERO From the story it is clear that Achilles is projected as the hero of the Iliad, though Hector, the Trojan warrior is in confrontation with him and in seeking a share of the heroic status. Both are well matched but the claim to be the Homeric hero seems to belong to Achilles as the entire story hinges upon Achilles, who after his initial refusal to fight enters the battlefield to avenge the killing of his friend Patroclus . Achille aS warrior, as a friend, as a valiant fighter who sets aside his pride and hurt ego for the higher cause of avenging his friend s death and finally as a magnanimous and compassionate human being in the last book (Book XXIV) when he returns Hectors body to Hectors old father to enable him to give his son a befitting hero s burial- represents all the heroic ideals of the Homeric age. The next Unit (Unit III War in the Homeric Age: Honour Vs Horror- ) will discuss the War concept prevalent at that time. The poem after announcing the theme (the wrath, or the anger of Achilles and its effects) and invoking the Muse goes straight to the conflict between Agamemnon and Achilles. Achilles pride and hurt ego initially make him stay away from the war But more distressing almost to the point of betrayal is his prayer to his mother, the goddess Thetis, to request Zeus to ensure a Trojan victory over his own countrymen, the Greeks. His non participation in the crucial war On a perceived slight to his ego and his treacherous scheme to take revenge 0n Agamemnon for insulting his honour, by praying for the defeat of his own countrymen are two major flaws in his character that come perilously close to discrediting his heroic status. But, it has to be remembered that personal honour The Homeric Hero is of prime importance to a Homeric hero. While the honour of Homeric society depends on the personal valour of its heroes, personal honour as Achilles shows depends on one s own moral evaluation of his honour The Iliad raises the question as to whether war with its use of violence and force can be morally justified in the context
title: 2.0 OBJECTIVES

text:
2.3 HECTOR AND ACHILLES

The two great warriors, Hector and Achilles , the former a Trojan and the latter a Greek, are both heroes of their respective nations. In the initial stages of the story, it is Hector who is triumphant though, his victorious onslaught over the Greeks takes place when the Greeks are without the services of their best warrior, Achilles. This, in no way mitigates the heroism and valour of Hector. Hector is committed to his country Though outraged at Paris ' (his brother s) action of abducting Helen, Hector agrees to fight the Greeks who in their vengeful fury were overrunning his country. Knowing the Greeks to be formidable he has to be the one potent force to defeat them. Hector fulfills his role as the defender of Troy Hector is a good example of a hero whose great feats On the battlefield are a measure of his loyalty to his society though he personally and morally disapproves of Paris ' elopement with Helen that has triggered the war He shows character by his willingness to enter the battlefield though he recognises the inevitability of defeat and the subsequent suffering that will befall his wife and his son

Unlike Achilles , Hector has a family and has great devotion and love for his wife and son The Iliad has a tender scene early in Book VI when Hectors wife Andromache and his son Astynax plead with him not to be on the vanguard and plunge headlong into war Andromache anticipates her own fate in case Hector gets killed as she has already lost her entire family of father and seven brothers who were victims to the wrath of Achilles. She cries out to Hector:

Yow, Hector y0ऋ Are my father nOW, m) noble mother, 0 brother to०, and you are my husband, young and warm and strong Pity me please.

Hector foresees the terrible horror his wife and son will undergo in case of his defeat.

That is nothing nothing beside your agony when S0me brazen Argive hales you off in tearS. wrenching away your daz of light and freedom! Then far off in the land of Argos you mASt live labouring at 0 loom, aAt another woman 5 becr and cali, fetching water at some spring Messeis 0r Hyperia, resisting all the way But still Hector will not be deterred; he decides to fight and prior to that, he prays and wishes that one day his son will be the prince of the Trojans, their best fighter better even than his father a joy to his mother 5 heart Hector is seen as a family man with deep love for his wife and child. Compare him with Achilles , who also has his softer side towards his mother and his friend Patroclus . This brings us to an important who is the real hero of the Iliad? We shall try and examine that next.
title: 2.3 HECTOR AND ACHILLES

text:
2.4 WHO IS THE HERO OF THE ILIAD?

This is a central question of the Iliad as to who lays claim to the status of a hero Achilles or Hector? Both have their unique place in their respective kingdoms. But in their attitude towards war in their display of loyalty to their masters, in their strong bonding with their families, one detects a fairly large degree of unlikeness. OU Even when Hector is furious about his brother Paris ' abduction of Helen , he casts aside his anger and enters the battlefield to save the Trojans from the onslaught of the Greeks. Hector shows that he can control and overcome his honest feelings about the wrongdoing of his brother in the larger interest of saving the honour of his countrymen. In contrast Achilles is full of vengeful anger initially against Agamemnon and later against Hector for killing his friend OPLES Patroclus . Rage characterises every action of Achilles Thus, Hector emerges a$ a civilised hero while Achilles seems to be a personification of primitive brutality pride, vindictiveness and wrath. sonificaion ofpriniivRSITY For Hector , war is a necessary evil and he is aware of his defeat at the end. But being forced into the war he has no option but to fight valiantly and bravely Achilles , on the other hand lives and swears by war and violence as he sees in it the means to preserve his dignity and pride and earn personal and everlasting glory. Hector s approach is altruistic and humanistic. Achilles is self centred and savagely inhuman. Hector s loyalty is towards his people; Achilles ' loyalty is towards his friend. In fact, it is said that Hector is nobly and selflessly heroic. The loyalty integrity and love that Hector displays gives him an edge over Achilles to be the hero of the Iliad. But it is here one has to judge not by the humanistic values of our times but by the values of the Homeric world where to fight and win glory was the highest goal of a warrior Let us see what the heroic attributes of those days were According to Cole D Lemme, they were among other things, Honour; Respect for the Dead Battle and Bravery; Devotion to king or the leader and lastly, and more importantly Opinions concerning eternal glory and a short life versus no eternal glory and a long happy life. 19 Homer: The Iliad Achilles , the unrivalled Homeric hero exemplifies the first while Hector the brave man with great love and concern for his family is for a moment confounded by the latter Let us discuss the above mentioned heroic attributes of the Homeric world: Honour is a heroic ideal, all-embracing broad and comprehensive. It refers to ones reputation, glory upright character integrity dignity and pride. It is not that all great heroes of Homer have all these distinctive attributes, but they adhere to the basic and the essential aspects of the concept of honor Hector shows uprightness of character when he decides to fight a war that is provoked by an unethical act committed by his brother He १S १ man, devoted to his family caring husband, proud father १ trustworthy son, a loyal citizen and an honorable soldier Hector is more a man who loves a peaceful family life with his wife and son. His unflinching loyalty and devotion to his king and his country not rage or revenge, makes him fight unto death. The only reason Hector does not leave the army and live a long life with his wife is because he cannot face the disgrace of leaving Priam and the army in their hour of need and, being labelled a coward Hector saysः All this weighs 0n my mind too, dear wOmAn But [ would die of shame to face the men 0f Troy And the Trojan women trailing their long robes If 1 would shrink from battle nOW, A coward. The only reason Hector does not give up fighting even when he knows the odds are stacked against him is because he cannot take the disgrace of being called a coward. He tells his wife, that he will actually die of shame, and this thought of everyone remembering him as a coward is even more distressing than the pleasure of a peaceful life in the company of his wife and son Achilles, however is quite unconcerned about obeying the king s orders and only fights for his own reasons. Only when Achilles decideds to come to Troy to garner eternal glory does he come; only when he learns about Hector killing Patroclus , he enters the battlefield. He fights of his own volition without any regard or loyalty to king Agamemnon or any other king Achilles shows his pride when he refuses to fight for Agamemnon and that leaves
title: 2.4 WHO IS THE HERO OF THE ILIAD?

text:
2.5 LET US SUM UP

We have learnt that the story of the Iliad basically hinges on Achilles, his rage and his heroism. We have shown how Achilles is as great a hero in his OWn right as Hector his opponent We have compared and contrasted the two warriors Hector and Achilles We have analysed their strengths and weaknesses to help us identify who possibly can claim the status of the hero of the epic.
title: 2.5 LET US SUM UP
